metadata.full_court_name: Fast Track Special Court for Trial and Disposal of Rape and POCSO Act Cases, Ranga Reddy District at Rajendranagar
metadata.case_number_citations: Sessions Case No. 1172 of 2019 (Cr.No. 327 of 2016 of P.S. Rajendranagar)

parties.petitioner_appellant: The State of Telangana through the Sub Inspector of Police, P.S. Rajendranagar, Cyberabad
parties.respondent: Shaik Amir Pasha @ Omer S/o. Habeeb, Age: 25 yrs, Caste: Muslim, Occ: Driver, R/o. H. No. 1-2-149, Sai Krishna Colony, Budwel, Rajendranagar

principal_witnesses_and_ex_pw.key_testimony: Stated that she does not know the facts of the case and that her parents obtained her signature on a blank paper.
Stated that she does not know the facts of the case and that the accused never committed any offence.
Stated that he does not know the facts of the case and that the accused never committed any offence.
Provided evidence regarding the investigation conducted and the filing of the charge sheet against the accused.

summary.introduction: This case involves a Sessions Case (No. 1172 of 2019) tried by the Fast Track Special Court for Rape and POCSO Act cases in Ranga Reddy District, Rajendranagar. The accused, Shaik Amir Pasha @ Omer, a 25-year-old driver, was charged with stalking and sexual harassment of a minor student (PW1) under Section 354-D(1)(i) of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) and Section 11 read with Section 12 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012. The allegations stated that for the last six months prior to March 2016, the accused had been torturing the victim on her way to her school, Ravindera Vidyankethan at Budwel. Specific incidents included the accused catching hold of the victim while she was attending her SSC examination and making gestures while she was returning home. The case was registered as Crime No. 327 of 2016 at P.S. Rajendranagar.
summary.prosecution_plaintiffs_case: The prosecution's case relied primarily on the testimony of the victim (PW1), her mother (PW2), her brother (PW3), and the investigating officer (PW4). The prosecution alleged that the victim lodged a report on March 30, 2016, detailing the harassment. The investigation involved recording statements under Section 161 Cr.P.C. from the victim and her family, recording a statement under Section 164 Cr.P.C. from the victim before the Metropolitan Magistrate, and conducting a scene of offence panchanama. The prosecution submitted various exhibits including the FIR, the 164 Cr.P.C. statement, and signatures on reports. The core narrative presented was that despite the accused's parents scolding him after being informed by the victim's parents, the harassment continued, culminating in specific physical and verbal acts on the day of the SSC examination.
summary.defense_strategy: The accused, Shaik Amir Pasha, pleaded not guilty and claimed to be tried. During the trial, the defense strategy focused on challenging the credibility of the prosecution witnesses. The accused denied all incriminating material presented by the prosecution during his examination under Section 313 Cr.P.C. and reported no defense evidence. The defense successfully highlighted that the material witnesses—the victim, her mother, and her brother—turned hostile during their cross-examination. The victim specifically testified in court that her signature on the initial police report was obtained on a blank paper and that she did not know the facts of the case as alleged. Furthermore, the defense pointed out the absence of corroborative evidence such as CCTV footage, statements from other student eyewitnesses, or call data records to support the prosecution's version of events.
summary.ratio_decidendi: The Court held that the burden of proof lies with the prosecution to establish the guilt of the accused beyond all reasonable doubt. In cases involving sexual offenses against minors, the testimony of the victim is crucial, but it must be supported by corroborative evidence when the prosecution witnesses turn hostile. The Court found that the prosecution utterly failed to prove its case because the material witnesses (PW1, PW2, and PW3) declared themselves hostile and denied the allegations, stating they did not know the facts of the case. Since the victim's statement in court contradicted the police statement and she alleged coercion (signature on a blank paper), her testimony could not be relied upon. With the collapse of the prosecution's case due to the hostility of material witnesses and the total absence of independent corroborative evidence, the Court concluded that the prosecution had failed to discharge its burden of proof, necessitating the acquittal of the accused.
summary.final_holding: The Court found the accused not guilty of the offences punishable under Section 354-D(1)(i) of the Indian Penal Code and Section 11 read with Section 12 of the POCSO Act. Consequently, the accused was acquitted under Section 235(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure (Cr.P.C.). The Court ordered that the bail bonds of the accused shall remain in force for a period of six months as required under Section 437-A of the Cr.P.C. Additionally, the Court noted that since the victim did not support the alleged sexual harassment, she is not entitled to any compensation from the State.

timeline.title: Incident Occurred
timeline.excerpt: On 30-03-2016 she went to attend SSC examination at her school, Rajendranagar where the accused caught hold her and asked her to come out, but on the intervention of other students he went away and while she was returning home the accused gave gestures.
timeline.reasoning: The judgment explicitly states the date of the specific incident where the accused caught hold of the victim and made gestures.

timeline.title: FIR Registered
timeline.excerpt: The complainant and victim/LW1 lodged report with the Sub Inspector of Police/LW9 on 30-03-2016 at 02:30 a.m.... Basing on the said report, LW9 registered a case in Cr. No. 327/2016...
timeline.reasoning: The report was lodged on 30-03-2016, and the case was registered based on this report on the same day.

timeline.title: Arrest of Accused
timeline.excerpt: On 01-04-2016 he apprehended the accused and after arresting him he produced the accused before the court for judicial custody.
timeline.reasoning: The judgment explicitly mentions the date of apprehension and arrest as 01-04-2016.

timeline.title: Case Transferred to Fast Track Court
timeline.excerpt: By virtue of order Dated. 20-12-2021 vide Dis.No. 3170/2021, the Hon'ble Prl District and Sessions Judge, R.R.District at L.B.Nagar, transferred to this court for trial and disposal.
timeline.reasoning: The specific date of the transfer order moving the case to the current Fast Track Court is provided in the text.

timeline.title: Judgment Delivered (Acquittal)
timeline.excerpt: Typed to my dictation by Stenographer, corrected by me and pronounced by me in the open Court, on this the 08th day of February, 2024... In the result, the accused is found not guilty... and accordingly, he is acquitted U/Sec.235(1) Cr.P.C
timeline.reasoning: The judgment was pronounced on 08-02-2024, resulting in the acquittal of the accused.

police.lapse: Obtaining signature on a blank paper by the victim (PW1) instead of recording a statement under Section 161 CrPC or Section 164 CrPC with proper safeguards.
police.impact: Destroyed the credibility of the victim's initial complaint and the subsequent 164 CrPC statement, leading the victim to claim the statement was fabricated or coerced, directly causing her to turn hostile.
police.reasoning: This is a fundamental violation of procedural safeguards for minors. Recording a statement on a blank paper renders the evidence legally suspect and inadmissible as a truthful account of the incident, violating principles of natural justice and the specific protections required for POCSO cases.

police.lapse: Failure to record the victim's statement under Section 161 CrPC properly or ensure independent recording, relying solely on a statement obtained via a blank paper.
police.impact: The primary evidence of the crime (the victim's account) became unreliable, leaving the prosecution with no independent corroboration of the alleged harassment.
police.reasoning: Under CrPC, statements of victims, especially minors, must be recorded meticulously. The failure to do so resulted in a complete evidentiary vacuum regarding the core allegation.

police.lapse: Failure to secure independent corroboration such as CCTV footage, statements of other students, or Call Data Records (CDR).
police.impact: The prosecution's case stood entirely on the testimony of the victim, her mother, and brother, all of whom turned hostile. Without independent witnesses or physical evidence, the case collapsed.
police.reasoning: In cases involving allegations of stalking or harassment where direct physical evidence is scarce, the law requires strong corroborative evidence to sustain a conviction. The total absence of such evidence is a critical investigative lapse.

prosecution.lapse: Failure to cross-examine the investigating officer (PW4) effectively regarding the method of obtaining the victim's signature on the blank paper.
prosecution.impact: The prosecution failed to explain away the glaring procedural irregularity, allowing the defense to successfully undermine the entire case.
prosecution.reasoning: The Special Public Prosecutor admitted the material witnesses turned hostile and gave up the remaining witnesses, failing to present any counter-evidence or explanation for the police misconduct.

lapses.judicial_criticism: The Court did not explicitly criticize the Investigating Officer (PW4) by name in the judgment text provided, but the acquittal was explicitly based on the finding that the prosecution failed to prove guilt due to the victim turning hostile and the lack of corroborative evidence. The judgment implicitly condemns the investigation by stating 'the evidence of the police official is of no help to the case of the prosecution' when material witnesses turn hostile due to the alleged blank paper signature.

severity.rationale: The investigation suffers from a 'Severe' lapse (Score 8). The core issue is the obtaining of a signature on a blank paper, which is a fatal flaw in the evidence chain for a POCSO case involving a minor. This specific lapse directly caused the victim to turn hostile, destroying the primary evidence. While not a deliberate fabrication (Score 9), it represents a gross negligence that rendered the investigation useless without independent corroboration, which was entirely absent. The case failed because the investigation created the only evidence available, which was then invalidated.

lapses.perfect_chain_of_evidence: The chain of evidence was completely broken. The prosecution relied exclusively on the testimony of the victim (PW1), her mother (PW2), and brother (PW3). All three turned hostile, with the victim explicitly stating her statement was obtained on a blank paper. There was no independent corroboration (no CCTV, no other student witnesses, no forensic evidence, no CDRs) to support the allegations. Consequently, the prosecution could not prove the guilt of the accused beyond reasonable doubt, leading to acquittal.
